title: The Nervous System and Mindful Releasing: How Letting Go Physically Heals Trauma
excerpt: Discover how mindful physical releasing calms the nervous system and promotes trauma healing. Learn science-backed methods to let go and reclaim your body.
body:
Understanding Trauma Beyond the Mind

When most people think of trauma, they think of memories, emotions, or flashbacks — things that happen in the mind. But trauma is as much a body story as it is a mental one, a fact that leading researchers like Bessel van der Kolk have emphasized. In The Body Keeps the Score, van der Kolk highlights how trauma imprints itself deeply in the nervous system and body, often below conscious awareness. This means that healing trauma isn’t just about talking or thinking; it’s about engaging the body and nervous system to release what’s stuck.

Somatic healing approaches focus on this body-based release, helping people who still feel stuck after traditional therapy. This article explores how the nervous system stores trauma, why mindful releasing matters, and practical, science-based ways to physically let go for lasting healing.

The Nervous System’s Role in Trauma

The nervous system is the body’s communication network, regulating everything from heart rate to digestion to emotional responses. Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory is especially useful here. It describes how the autonomic nervous system (ANS) has three primary states:

  • Ventral Vagal: The social engagement system, where we feel safe and connected.
  • Sympathetic: The fight-or-flight response, preparing us to respond to danger.
  • Dorsal Vagal: The freeze or shutdown response, often activated when fight or flight isn’t possible.

Trauma can lock the nervous system into sympathetic hyperarousal (anxiety, panic) or dorsal vagal hypoarousal (numbness, dissociation). This dysregulation is why trauma survivors often experience physical symptoms — tight muscles, gastrointestinal issues, or chronic pain — even when they are not consciously thinking about their trauma.

Why Talking Isn’t Enough

Psychotherapy is invaluable, but as Pat Ogden points out in her work on Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, cognitive approaches don’t always reach the somatic imprint of trauma. The body holds implicit memories — patterns of tension, posture, or breath — that words alone can’t fully access or change.

For example, a person may intellectually understand they are safe now, but their body might still react as if danger is present. This mismatch causes ongoing distress and symptoms.

What is Mindful Releasing?

Mindful releasing is a practice of gently tuning into bodily sensations, noticing where tension or holding exists, and allowing the body to soften and release that tension with curiosity and care. Unlike forcing or pushing, it’s about listening to the body and creating safety for natural discharge.

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, describes this as enabling the completion of survival responses that trauma interrupted. When fight, flight, or freeze responses are curtailed, energy gets trapped. Mindful releasing helps discharge that energy physically, which calms the nervous system.

“Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you.” – Peter Levine

Key Components of Mindful Releasing

  • Awareness: Paying attention to body sensations without judgment.
  • Safety: Creating an environment where the nervous system can relax.
  • Gentle Movement or Stillness: Allowing natural tension release, such as shaking, stretching, or deep breathing.
  • Patience: Letting the body release in its own time, not forcing outcomes.

How Mindful Releasing Physically Heals Trauma

When practiced regularly, mindful releasing engages the parasympathetic nervous system, particularly through the ventral vagal complex, promoting a sense of safety and restoration. Deb Dana, a leading clinician specialized in Polyvagal Theory, emphasizes that restoring this ventral vagal tone is critical for healing trauma.

Here’s what happens physiologically during mindful releasing:

  • Dampening of Sympathetic Activation: Physical release reduces adrenaline and cortisol levels, decreasing fight-or-flight symptoms.
  • Reduction of Muscle Guarding: Chronic tension held as a protective mechanism softens, reducing pain and improving movement.
  • Improved Interoception: Increased awareness of internal bodily states helps regulate emotions better.
  • Restoration of Autonomic Balance: The nervous system shifts toward a calm, regulated state, reducing triggers and hypervigilance.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model also aligns here, suggesting that parts of us hold trauma in the body, and mindful somatic work helps those parts feel safe enough to unburden their pain.

Practical Mindful Releasing Techniques

If you’ve tried therapy and still feel stuck, these accessible practices grounded in research can help you start releasing trauma physically — on your own terms.

1. Body Scan with Breath Awareness

Start by settling into a comfortable position. Close your eyes and take slow, deep breaths. Bring focused attention to different areas of your body, noticing any tension or discomfort without trying to change it. Breathe into those sensations, inviting them to soften.

This practice builds interoception, a key skill in somatic healing identified by Dan Siegel, helping you tune into your body’s messages.

2. Grounding Through the Feet

Stand or sit with your feet flat on the floor. Feel the contact points, the weight distribution, and the texture under your feet. Imagine roots growing from your feet deep into the earth. This simple grounding activates the ventral vagal system, helping regulate your nervous system.

3. Gentle Tremoring or Shaking

Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing often uses involuntary tremors or shaking as an expression of nervous system discharge. You can invite your body to shake gently — arms, legs, or the whole body — in a safe space. If shaking arises naturally, let it happen without judgment.

This movement releases stored survival energy and calms hyperarousal.

4. Progressive Muscle Relaxation with Mindful Attention

Sequentially tense and then release muscle groups while maintaining awareness of sensations. Unlike quick relaxation, this method deepens the nervous system’s capacity to shift from tension to ease.

5. Self-Soothing Touch

Gently placing your hands on your heart, belly, or arms can stimulate the parasympathetic nervous system. Deb Dana highlights the power of safe touch in regulating nervous system responses.

Try slow, circular motions or simply hold your hands still, appreciating the warmth and connection.

When to Seek Support

While self-directed somatic work is powerful, trauma can be complex. If mindful releasing triggers overwhelming emotions, flashbacks, or dissociation, consider working with a trauma-informed somatic therapist. Francine Shapiro’s EMDR and guided somatic therapies can provide safety and structure for deeper healing.

Also, start slowly and build your capacity over time. Gabor Maté advises that pacing is key—healing is a process, not a race.

Conclusion

Trauma lives in the body as much as in the mind. The nervous system holds on to what the brain cannot fully process, causing ongoing distress. Mindful releasing offers a science-backed path to healing by engaging the body’s natural capacity to discharge, regulate, and restore balance.

If you’ve felt stuck despite therapy, consider incorporating these somatic practices into your healing journey. By listening deeply to your body and allowing physical release with care and patience, you can reclaim your nervous system’s resilience and find more lasting peace.

“Healing doesn’t mean the damage never existed. It means the damage no longer controls our lives.” – Bessel van der Kolk

faqs.q: Why does trauma affect the body and not just the mind?
faqs.a: Trauma impacts the autonomic nervous system, causing physical symptoms like tension or pain. The body stores implicit memories that are separate from conscious thought, making somatic healing essential.

faqs.q: What is the difference between mindful releasing and traditional relaxation?
faqs.a: Mindful releasing involves active, aware attention to bodily sensations and allowing natural release of tension, whereas traditional relaxation often focuses on passive muscle relaxation without deep somatic engagement.

faqs.q: Can I practice mindful releasing without a therapist?
faqs.a: Yes, many techniques like body scans, grounding, and gentle shaking can be safely practiced at home. However, if intense emotions arise, seeking professional guidance is recommended.

faqs.q: How does Polyvagal Theory inform trauma healing practices?
faqs.a: Polyvagal Theory explains how the nervous system’s different states affect our sense of safety and connection. Understanding these states helps tailor somatic interventions to restore autonomic balance.
